relevant_files.role: Primary security scanning logic that processes diffs line-by-line using PatternEngine
PatternEngine that loads and executes regex-based security patterns from patterns.json
Embedded pattern database with 14+ regex patterns for vulnerability detection
Integration tests including test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected documenting known limitation
Test fixture demonstrating SQL injection patterns that work with current line-based scanner
Workspace configuration showing Rust 1.92.0 and resolver = 3

conventions.commits: Conventional commits enforced via .commitlintrc.yml using @commitlint/config-conventional (feat, fix, chore, docs, test, etc.)
conventions.testing: Comprehensive unit and integration tests; tests located in crates/*/tests/ and tests/ directories; CI runs cargo test, fmt --check, and clippy -- -D warnings
conventions.linting: Clippy with pedantic lints enabled but allowing module_name_repetitions, missing_errors_doc, missing_panics_doc; rustfmt for formatting
conventions.error_handling: Uses thiserror and anyhow for error handling; tracing for logging; backon for retry logic
conventions.import_module_organization: Workspace with resolver = 3; clear module separation (security, github, ai, etc.); lib.rs exports public API

patterns: Lazy static initialization of PatternEngine using LazyLock for global access pattern
Embedded JSON patterns loaded at compile time via include_str! for no runtime file I/O
Line-by-line scanning with file extension filtering to avoid false positives across languages
Diff-aware scanning that tracks file changes and line numbers from unified diff format
Ignore patterns via SecurityConfig for path-based and pattern-based exclusions

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection
related_issues.relevance: PRIMARY - The issue requesting tree-sitter evaluation for multi-line vulnerability detection

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: HIGH - Documents the known limitation that scanner cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities where source and sink are on different lines

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation (PR)
related_issues.relevance: HIGH - Implements the test case documenting the limitation that multi-line SQL injection is not detected by current regex-based scanner

constraints: Current SecurityScanner operates line-by-line, making multi-line vulnerability detection impossible with regex patterns
PatternEngine uses embedded patterns.json with regex-based patterns, requiring line-by-line scanning
Workspace uses Rust 1.92.0 and resolver = 3, which must be compatible with any new dependencies
Binary size and dependency count must be considered when adding tree-sitter grammars (5-10 languages estimated)
Incremental parsing requirement: tree-sitter must efficiently handle file edits in diffs
test_coverage: Comprehensive test coverage for current regex-based scanner including: unit tests in patterns.rs (9 tests), integration tests in security_integration.rs (4 tests), and fixture-based testing. Current tests verify detection of hardcoded secrets, SQL injection via concatenation and format strings, command injection, weak crypto (MD5, SHA1), path traversal, and XSS. Negative test explicitly documents the multi-line limitation (test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected).

library_findings.relevant_api: Parser::new(), set_language(), parse(), root_node(), Query::new(), query.captures() for pattern matching
library_findings.notes: Tree-sitter provides incremental parsing, language grammars for Rust/Python/JS/Java/etc., and S-expression query syntax. The Rust API is mature and stable. Query system allows capturing nodes like function calls, string concatenation, variable assignments across multiple lines.

Official Rust grammar for tree-sitter. Must match tree-sitter version (0.26.7). Provides syntax tree for Rust code enabling AST-based pattern matching for vulnerabilities like SQL injection across multiple lines.

approaches.name: Hybrid Scanner: Regex + Tree-Sitter
approaches.description: Maintain current regex-based PatternEngine for simple patterns (single-line) while adding a new AST-based PatternEngine for multi-line patterns. Create a unified SecurityScanner that routes to appropriate engine based on pattern type. Keep patterns.json for regex patterns and add new tree-sitter query files (.scm) for AST patterns.
pros: Minimal disruption to existing architecture and tests
Gradual migration path with clear separation of concerns
Reuses existing pattern database and infrastructure
Avoids breaking changes to current users
Performance: regex for simple patterns, tree-sitter for complex multi-line patterns
Allows incremental adoption by pattern type
cons: Maintains two parallel scanning systems increasing code complexity
Still requires tree-sitter grammars for each language (5-10 languages)
Pattern conversion effort: 14 patterns need analysis for which should be AST-based
Binary size increase from additional dependencies

approaches.name: Full Migration to Tree-Sitter
approaches.description: Replace the entire regex-based PatternEngine with a tree-sitter based solution. Create a new ASTPatternEngine that uses tree-sitter queries instead of regex. Convert all 14+ patterns to tree-sitter queries. Update SecurityScanner to use the new engine. Requires adding tree-sitter and language grammars to Cargo.toml.
pros: Full detection capability including multi-line vulnerabilities
Language-aware parsing provides better context and fewer false positives
Modern AST-based approach better suited for data-flow analysis
Opportunity to refactor and improve overall architecture
Future-proof foundation for advanced security analysis
cons: High disruption to existing codebase and tests
Requires complete rewrite of pattern matching logic
All 14 patterns must be converted to tree-sitter queries (learning curve for Scheme-like syntax)
Must maintain backward compatibility during migration
Binary size increase and longer compile times

approaches.name: Language-Specific AST Scanners
approaches.description: Create separate AST-based scanners for each major language (Rust, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript). Implement a LanguageScanner trait with default implementations. Keep regex-based scanner for simple patterns and add language-specific AST scanners only where needed. Use a factory pattern to select the appropriate scanner based on file extension.
pros: Targeted approach: only add AST scanning where it provides value
Minimizes dependency bloat by adding grammars only for languages in use
Allows gradual adoption by language rather than by pattern
Clear separation of concerns between languages
Easier to maintain and test individual language scanners
cons: Fragmented architecture with multiple scanning strategies
Complex routing logic to determine which scanner to use
Potential code duplication across language scanners
Harder to implement cross-language vulnerability patterns
Still requires tree-sitter grammars for each target language

recommendation: Hybrid Scanner: Regex + Tree-Sitter. This approach provides the best balance between immediate value (multi-line detection) and minimal disruption. It allows the team to evaluate tree-sitter incrementally while maintaining existing functionality. The hybrid approach reduces risk and provides a clear migration path. Start with implementing tree-sitter-rust for the SQL injection pattern (source and sink on different lines) as a spike, then expand to other patterns and languages based on findings.
